1.note: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God... all things were made through him.' John's Gospel opens with the same words as Genesis 1:1 (en arche / bereshit) deliberately, identifying the Word (Logos) as the divine agent of the original creation. Genesis 1:1 is the seed John 1:1-3 germinates — the same 'beginning,' the same creative activity, now personalized in Christ

'All things were created through him and for him... in him all things hold together.' Paul's cosmic Christology reads the Genesis 1 creation as mediated through the Son; the 'all things' of v1 (heavens and earth) is the same 'all things' now attributed to Christ's agency as the one through whom and for whom creation exists

'Through whom he also made the ages (tous aionas).' Hebrews describes the Son as the agent of creation, echoing Genesis 1:1 — the 'beginning' of creation is the beginning of the Son's creative work. The plural 'ages' expands Genesis' temporal frame to include all epochs of history as created through the Son

In the beginning God created — John 1:1 deliberately echoes Genesis 1:1 (en arche = bereshit); what God did in Genesis 1:1 through the word, the Word (Logos) was present to do; the new creation of the gospel is narrated in the same opening cadence as the first creation
The first heaven and earth (Genesis 1:1) will pass away and be replaced by new heavens and new earth; Revelation's new creation is the eschatological bookend to Genesis's original creation

2.note: 'The Spirit of God descending like a dove' at Jesus's baptism echoes the Spirit of God hovering (merachefet) over the primordial waters in Genesis 1:2. Matthew's dove-descending is a new-creation signal: just as the Spirit hovered over the formless chaos before creation began, the Spirit's descent at baptism marks the beginning of the new creation in Christ

Jesus breathes on the disciples and says 'Receive the Holy Spirit' — a deliberate echo of both Genesis 1:2 (Spirit over the waters) and Genesis 2:7 (YHWH breathing life into Adam). John's new-creation Pentecost re-enacts the original creation/animation: as the Spirit hovered over the pre-creation void, the risen Christ breathes the eschatological life of the Spirit into the new-creation community

3.note: 'God who said, Let light shine out of darkness, has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.' Paul reads the Genesis 1:3 creation-light command as a type fulfilled in conversion: as the primal darkness was pierced by the divine word 'let there be light,' so the spiritual darkness of the unregenerate heart is pierced by the gospel of Christ's glory

'In him was life, and that life was the light of all mankind. The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.' John's light-in-darkness language is structurally continuous with Genesis 1:3-4: the Word who was the agent of the original light-creation is himself the light in the Johannine prologue; the separation of light from darkness in Genesis is now the cosmic drama of the Word's coming into the world
Let there be light — God shines his light in our hearts

26.note: 'He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation.' Paul's Christological application of the imago dei: Christ is not merely made 'in' the image but IS the image (eikon) of which Genesis 1:26-27 speaks. The human creature made in the divine image is a derivative of the Son who is the original image; Christ is the archetype, humanity the ectype

'The god of this age has blinded the minds of unbelievers, so that they cannot see the light of the gospel that displays the glory of Christ, who is the image of God.' Paul's identification of Christ as the image of God (eikon tou theou) in the evangelistic context applies the Genesis 1:26-27 language to Christ: the glory the Fall obscured is the glory that shines in Christ's face

'And just as we have borne the image of the earthly man, so shall we bear the image of the heavenly man.' The eschatological restoration of the imago dei: the image of the dusty Adam (the creational image now marred by sin) will be replaced by the image of the heavenly Christ. Genesis 1:26-27 establishes the destiny; 1 Corinthians 15:49 announces its eschatological recovery

Made in the image of God (imago Dei) — humanity's creation in the divine image is the basis for the new-creation renewal in Colossians: the new self is being renewed in knowledge after the image of its creator; the image lost in the Fall is restored in Christ

Predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son — God's original purpose in creating humanity in his image (Genesis 1:26) is eschatologically completed in conformity to the image of Christ, the perfect image-bearer
Made in the image of God — Christ the image of the invisible God

27.note: 'Have you not read that he who created them from the beginning made them male and female?' Jesus cites Genesis 1:27 in his divorce teaching, appealing to the creational design ('from the beginning') as the norm that supersedes Mosaic concession. The male-female binary of Genesis 1:27 is the hermeneutical starting point for Jesus's teaching on marriage's permanence

Mark's parallel account of Jesus's divorce teaching also cites Genesis 1:27 explicitly. Both Matthew and Mark record Jesus grounding the permanence of marriage in the creation ordinance — the two sexes as a divine design feature, not a cultural accident

'With the tongue we curse human beings, who have been made in God's likeness.' James grounds the moral prohibition against cursing in the imago dei of Genesis 1:26-27 — image-bearers are not to be cursed because cursing an image-bearer attacks the one whose image it is
Made them male and female -- Jesus on marriage and divorce

28.note: 'You put everything under their feet' — the author of Hebrews cites Psalms 8:6 but reads it against the Genesis 1:28 commission to subdue and rule. The Psalm-citation is applied to Christ: what Genesis 1:28 commissioned (humanity ruling creation) and what Psalm 8 celebrated (human dignity) is now fulfilled in the exalted Christ who has all things under his feet — but the 'not yet' of universal subjection awaits

2.note: 'He has spoken in a certain place about the seventh day in these words: On the seventh day God rested from all his works.' Hebrews quotes Genesis 2:2 as the foundation for its extended argument about the 'sabbath rest' that remains for the people of God. The divine rest of the seventh day is both the prototype of the promised land-rest and the anticipation of the eschatological rest that believers enter through faith in Christ

'Do not let anyone judge you by what you eat or drink, or with regard to a religious festival, a New Moon celebration or a Sabbath day. These are a shadow of the things that were to come; the reality, however, is found in Christ.' The sabbath of Genesis 2:2-3 is here called a 'shadow' — a forward-pointing provisional form — whose substance/body (soma) is Christ. Genesis 2:2's rest is the shadow; Christ is the reality it was pointing to
God rested on the seventh day from all his works

7.note: 'So it is written: The first man Adam became a living being; the last Adam, a life-giving spirit.' Paul quotes Genesis 2:7 LXX exactly ('the man became a living being' = egeneto ho anthropos eis psychen zosan) as the protological basis for the Adam-Christ typology. The 'first Adam' of Genesis 2:7 receives life from YHWH; the 'last Adam' gives life eschatologically. Paul reads Genesis 2:7 as the thesis statement of the creation-order that Christ fulfills and transcends

'The first man was of the dust of the earth; the second man is of heaven.' Paul's Adam-from-dust language directly depends on Genesis 2:7 (aphar min-ha'adamah = dust from the ground). The earthly origin of the first man — formed from soil — contrasts with the heavenly origin of Christ; the material of creation defines the nature of the fall and the nature of the redemption

Jesus breathes on the disciples and says 'Receive the Holy Spirit' — a deliberate recall of YHWH breathing life (nishmat chayyim) into Adam in Genesis 2:7. As the original creation involved divine breath animating dust into a living being, the new creation involves the risen Christ breathing eschatological life into the community. John's Pentecost is a Genesis 2:7 re-enactment

YHWH formed man from the dust and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and man became a living being — Paul quotes Genesis 2:7 LXX exactly (psuche zosa) to contrast the first Adam (living soul) with the last Adam (life-giving spirit); the first creation's humanity is the baseline against which the new creation's humanity is measured
The first man Adam became a living being

9.note: 'To the one who is victorious, I will give the right to eat from the tree of life, which is in the paradise of God.' The tree of life in the garden of Eden (Genesis 2:9) reappears in the eschatological paradise as the reward for persevering faith. What was denied to Adam after the Fall (blocked by cherubim in Genesis 3:24) is restored to the overcomer in Christ

'On each side of the river stood the tree of life, bearing twelve crops of fruit, yielding its fruit every month.' The New Jerusalem's tree of life is the Eden tree of Genesis 2:9 restored and magnified: what was one tree in one garden becomes an abundant grove accessible to all nations. The end of Revelation consciously mirrors and surpasses the beginning of Genesis
Tree of life restored -- what was lost in Eden is regained

17.note: 'Just as sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death came to all people, because all sinned.' Paul's Adam-typology is grounded in the Genesis 2:17 command — 'in the day you eat of it you will surely die.' The entrance of death into the world traces back to this transgressed command; the one man through whom death came is the man who violated the Genesis 2:17 prohibition

'In Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive.' The universal scope of death-in-Adam derives from Genesis 2:17's threatened consequence: the death-sentence pronounced on one man (Adam) became the death-reality for all humanity. The Corinthian passage makes explicit what Genesis implies: all are 'in Adam,' all inherit the consequence of Genesis 2:17's violated prohibition

23.note: 'For no one ever hated their own body, but they feed and care for their body, just as Christ does the church — for we are members of his body.' Paul's argument develops from Genesis 2:23 (woman taken from man's body) and Genesis 2:24 (they become one flesh): the church-as-body-of-Christ mirrors the woman-taken-from-man's-body, and Christ's care for the church mirrors a man's care for his own body/flesh

'For man did not come from woman, but woman from man.' Paul's argument about head coverings draws on the creation sequence of Genesis 2:21-23 — the woman was taken from the man, not vice versa. The direction of creational derivation (woman from man) is Paul's warrant for the theological asymmetry he is establishing in the Corinthian context

24.note: Jesus cites Genesis 2:24 in the divorce controversy: 'A man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh.' Jesus attributes the Genesis 2:24 statement to God's design ('he who created them...said'), making the creation narrative the normative standard for marriage — what God joined together in Eden, humans must not separate

'For this reason a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh. This is a profound mystery — but I am talking about Christ and the church.' Paul quotes Genesis 2:24 and then declares that its ultimate referent is the Christ-church union. The mystery hidden in the creation narrative of Gen 2 is the eschatological union of Christ and his people

'Do you not know that he who unites himself with a prostitute is one with her in body? For it is said, The two will become one flesh.' Paul cites Genesis 2:24 to argue that sexual union creates a real metaphysical bond, not merely a casual physical contact. The one-flesh union of Genesis 2:24 is Paul's ground for the seriousness of sexual immorality: it unites believers with whoever they sleep with

A man shall leave his father and mother and hold fast to his wife and they shall become one flesh — Paul quotes Genesis 2:24 and calls it a profound mystery (mysterion mega) referring to Christ and the church; the one-flesh union of marriage is the type of Christ's union with the church
A man will leave his parents and be united to his wife
A man will leave his parents -- Jesus on the permanence of marriage
Two will become one flesh -- Jesus on divorce

1.note: 'I am afraid that just as Eve was deceived by the serpent's cunning, your minds may somehow be led astray from your sincere and pure devotion to Christ.' Paul uses the Genesis 3:1-6 serpent/Eve encounter as the paradigm for doctrinal deception — the serpent's craft (Genesis 3:1 arum) is the model for the false apostles' deception; the Corinthians are in danger of the same pattern of corrupted thinking that destroyed Eve's single-minded fidelity

'The great dragon was hurled down — that ancient serpent called the devil, or Satan, who leads the whole world astray.' Revelation's cosmic drama identifies the Genesis 3 serpent with the dragon, the devil, and Satan — the explicit identification that Genesis leaves implicit. The 'ancient serpent' designation (also Revelation 20:2) traces the adversary's history back to the Eden encounter

'He was a murderer from the beginning, not holding to the truth, for there is no truth in him.' Jesus's characterization of the devil as 'a murderer from the beginning' and a liar refers back to Genesis 3:1-6 — the serpent's first recorded speech is a lie ('you will not certainly die'); the murder-from-the-beginning echoes John 8:44's connection with the Fall as the origin of the devil's homicidal career

6.note: 'And Adam was not the one deceived; it was the woman who was deceived and became a sinner.' Paul's pastoral argument regarding teaching authority draws on the Fall sequence of Genesis 3:6 — Eve was deceived and ate; Adam was not deceived but sinned knowingly. The order and manner of the Fall (serpent → Eve → Adam) is Paul's argument; he distinguishes the deception of Eve from Adam's sin

'For just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners, so also through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous.' The disobedience Paul traces to 'the one man' (Adam) is the deliberate act of eating in Genesis 3:6: 'he ate it.' The Adam-Christ parallel hinges on this moment of one man's decisive choice determining the destiny of many

15.note: 'But when the set time had fully come, God sent his Son, born of a woman, born under the law.' Paul's 'born of a woman' echoes the Genesis 3:15 'offspring of the woman' — the promised seed-of-the-woman who will crush the serpent is specifically born of woman (not of man alone), and Jesus's birth fulfills the proto-evangelion. The woman's seed becomes the Seed born of Mary

'The God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet.' Paul's closing promise is a direct echo of Genesis 3:15 ('he will crush your head') — the same crushing action, the same adversary. What God promised in Eden (the woman's seed crushing the serpent's head) is now promised to the Roman church: the eschatological crushing of Satan is imminent, mediated through the community of Christ

'The reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the devil's work.' 1 John interprets the Incarnation teleologically against the Genesis 3:15 background: the Son's appearance was specifically purposed to undo what the serpent's work began in Eden. The 'work of the devil' (deception, sin, death) introduced in Genesis 3 is the problem Christ's coming was designed to solve

'Since the children have flesh and blood, he too shared in their humanity so that by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death — that is, the devil.' Hebrews frames the Incarnation as the necessary step for defeating the death-power that traces back to Genesis 3:15's adversary; the crushing of the serpent required the Seed to share human mortality, taking the serpent's strike on himself to accomplish the crushing blow

That ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan, who deceives the whole world — Revelation's identification of the serpent as Satan retroactively clarifies who the Gen 3 serpent was; the Genesis 3:15 enmity reaches its climax in Rev 12's cosmic conflict
Seed of the woman — born of a woman under the law
Seed of woman crushes the serpent — God will crush Satan
Ancient serpent — the dragon, that old serpent, cast down

17.note: 'For the creation was subjected to frustration (mataioteti), not by its own choice, but by the will of the one who subjected it, in hope... the whole creation has been groaning as in the pains of childbirth.' Paul's creation-frustration/groaning directly echoes Genesis 3:17-19 — the ground cursed because of Adam, thorns and thistles, sweated labor, death-return-to-dust. The cosmic curse of Genesis 3:17-19 is Paul's datum for creation's present subjection to futility

'Sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death came to all people.' The death that entered through the one man (Adam) is the death threatened in Genesis 2:17 and fulfilled in Genesis 3:19 ('for dust you are and to dust you shall return'). Genesis 3:17-19 is the empirical confirmation of Genesis 2:17's warning; Romans 5:12 is the theological analysis of the universal consequence

19.note: 'The first man was of the dust of the earth.' Paul's Adam-from-dust language (1 Corinthians 15:45-47) draws simultaneously on Genesis 2:7 (formed from dust) and Genesis 3:19 ('for dust you are and to dust you shall return'). The dusty origin and the dusty destiny belong together in Paul's argument: the Christ who comes from heaven transforms the dust-destiny that the Adam-narrative establishes

'In Adam all die' — the universal death-in-Adam derives from Genesis 3:19's 'you will return to the ground' applied to all Adam's descendants. The death-sentence of 3:19 is the grounding claim for Paul's 'in Adam all die'; the one man's death-doom becomes the common lot of humanity

21.note: 'Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness.' The first animal deaths in the biblical narrative occur in Genesis 3:21 — God kills animals to provide skin-coverings for the sinful pair. The first blood is shed not by humans but by God, to cover human shame. Hebrews' principle that forgiveness requires blood-shedding is illustrated in germ by this first act of sacrificial covering; the pattern established in Eden runs through the Levitical sacrificial system to Christ

'They have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb.' The garments of skin God provided in Genesis 3:21 (covering the shame of sin) are the distant type of the white robes of the redeemed in Revelation. Both involve divine provision of covering for the shame of sin; both involve death (animal/Lamb) as the means of covering. The trajectory from skin-garments to white robes passes through the atoning death of Christ
God covered them with skins — without blood no forgiveness

22.note: 'Blessed are those who wash their robes, that they may have the right to the tree of life and may go through the gates into the city.' Genesis 3:22-24 narrates the expulsion from the garden and the blocking of access to the tree of life. Revelation 22 narrates the reversal: access to the tree of life is restored to those washed in the Lamb's blood. What the cherubim blocked in Genesis, the Lamb's atoning work reopens in Revelation

'We have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place by the blood of Jesus, by a new and living way opened for us through the curtain.' The curtain/veil blocking access to the divine presence in the tabernacle corresponds to the cherubim and flaming sword blocking re-entry to Eden in Genesis 3:24. Hebrews' announcement that the way into the Most Holy Place is now open through Christ's blood is the reversal of the Genesis 3:24 exclusion

24.note: 'I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.' After the Fall, the way to the tree of life was blocked (Genesis 3:24 — the cherubim and flaming sword). Jesus's 'I am the way' declaration implicitly answers Genesis 3:24's blocked path: the return to the life of God, of which the tree of life was the symbol, is now opened through Christ who is himself the way

3.note: The way of Cain — murder from envy

4.note: By faith Abel brought God a better offering than Cain did. By faith he still speaks, even though he is dead — the NT retrospect on the Cain/Abel offering reads Abel's as an act of faith and Cain's as faithless. The distinction Genesis draws (fat portions from the firstborn vs. some of the fruits without qualification) becomes the canonical grounding for the sacrificial principle that faith determines the quality of the gift.

All the fat is the LORD's; this is a lasting ordinance for the generations to come — the Levitical law that the fat belongs to YHWH echoes Abel's prototype offering of fat portions from the firstborn. Abel's instinct to give the best and richest portion anticipates the sacrificial code that institutionalizes the same principle centuries later.

Not like Cain who was of the evil one and murdered his brother — Cain becomes the NT type of the world's murderous hatred of the righteous; the community that faces hatred for righteousness stands where Abel stood

7.note: Do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its evil desires — Paul's imperative against sin reigning (basileuo) maps onto the divine warning to Cain that sin is crouching at the door and desires to have you, but you must rule over it. Both texts locate the decisive battle in the will's response to the personified, desire-language of sin seeking mastery over the human agent.

Your enemy the devil prowls around like a roaring lion looking for someone to devour — the image of sin crouching (Hebrew: robets, the posture of a predator lying in wait) is the same as Peter's prowling lion. Both texts use predatory-animal imagery to describe the posture of moral danger waiting to strike.

8.note: Do not be like Cain, who belonged to the evil one and murdered his brother. And why did he murder him? Because his own actions were evil and his brother's were righteous — the NT canonical reading identifies Cain's murder as flowing from spiritual allegiance (evil one) and the contrast between evil works and righteous works. The Genesis narrative of the rejected offering provides the backstory that 1 John reads as paradigmatic for sibling-hatred in the community.

Woe to them! They have taken the way of Cain — Jude uses the way of Cain as one of three OT archetypes for the false teachers (Cain, Balaam, Korah). The way of Cain is the path of offering without faith, of envy that becomes violence, and of the refusal to hear divine correction before the sin crouches and springs.

10.note: To Jesus the mediator of a new covenant, and to the sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel — the blood of Abel crying out from the ground is the canonical precedent that Hebrews invokes to describe the blood of Christ. Abel's blood spoke accusation and demanded justice; Christ's blood speaks forgiveness and provides atonement. Both bloods speak from the ground, but with opposite valence.

How long, Sovereign Lord, holy and true, until you judge the inhabitants of the earth and avenge our blood? — the martyrs under the altar in Revelation cry out in the same category as Abel's blood crying from the ground: murdered righteous blood demanding divine vindication. The blood-cry motif of Genesis 4:10 becomes the paradigm for all martyrdom-vindication language in the canon.

14.note: Do not cast me from your presence or take your Holy Spirit from me — David's plea not to be driven from the divine presence uses the same fear Cain voices in Genesis 4:14: I will be hidden from your presence. Both texts treat exclusion from the divine presence as the ultimate punishment; David prays not to receive what Cain suffered.

23.note: I tell you, not seven times, but seventy-seven times — Jesus's instruction to forgive 77 times (or 70 times 7) is a deliberate inversion of Lamech's boast: if Cain is avenged seven times, then Lamech seventy-seven times. The Lamech escalation (from 7x divine protection of Cain to 77x self-vindication) represents the metastasis of violence; Jesus sets the anti-Lamech number as the pattern for the new community.

26.note: Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved — Peter's Pentecost citation of Joel 2:32 stands on the foundation laid in Genesis 4:26: at that time people began to call on the name of the LORD. Seth's line, not Cain's, is the line that worships, and the calling on the divine name that begins here runs through Joel's prophecy to Peter's proclamation.

1.note: This is the genealogy (biblios geneseos) of Jesus the Messiah the son of David, the son of Abraham — Matthew's opening formula is the Greek rendering of Genesis's sefer toledot adam (this is the written account of Adam's family line). By deliberately using the same phrase, Matthew signals that his book is a new Genesis: the genealogy of Jesus recapitulates and surpasses the genealogy of Adam.

The son of Enosh, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God — Luke's genealogy traces Jesus back through Genesis 5 to Adam and ultimately to God. The sefer toledot adam that begins in Genesis 5:1 culminates in Luke's genealogy by identifying Adam as son of God, making Jesus the last Adam who restores what the first Adam lost.

3.note: Just as we have borne the image of the earthly man, so shall we bear the image of the heavenly man — Genesis 5:3 establishes that Seth was born in Adam's own likeness and image. The transmission of the image through the Adamic line (Genesis 5:3) is the context for Paul's contrast: we inherit the fallen image of the earthly Adam, but resurrection transforms us into the image of the heavenly Christ.

24.note: By faith Enoch was taken from this life, so that he did not experience death; he could not be found, because God had taken him away — Hebrews reads Enoch's translation as an act of faith: walking faithfully with God (Genesis 5:22, 24) is interpreted as a faith-condition that resulted in divine approval and bodily transformation. Enoch's translation becomes a type of resurrection hope.

Enoch, the seventh from Adam, prophesied about them: See, the Lord is coming with thousands upon thousands of his holy ones to judge everyone — Jude cites a saying attributed to Enoch (from 1 Enoch Genesis 1:9) as genuine prophecy. The canonical Enoch who walked with God and was taken becomes in Second Temple tradition the exemplary prophet who foresaw the final judgment.

1.note: Angels who sinned confined to darkness
Angels who abandoned their position

2.note: God did not spare angels when they sinned, but sent them to hell, putting them in chains of darkness to be held for judgment — 2 Peter reads the sons of God who married the daughters of humans (Genesis 6:2) as angelic beings who transgressed the boundary between heavenly and human realms. The Noahic judgment narrative is partially explained by this supernatural transgression preceding it.

The angels who did not keep their positions of authority but abandoned their proper dwelling — these he has kept in darkness, bound with everlasting chains for judgment on the great Day — Jude, like 2 Peter, reads Genesis 6:2 as describing supernatural beings (sons of God = angels) who violated their created order. The sons of God narrative is the genesis of a canonical tradition about fallen angelic beings.

5.note: The heart is deceitful above all things and beyond cure. Who can understand it? — Jeremiah's diagnosis of the human heart's radical corruption is the prophetic articulation of what Genesis 6:5 narrates: every inclination of the thoughts of the human heart was only evil all the time. The yetzer ha-ra (evil inclination) that fills Genesis 6 becomes the theological premise for the prophetic call to repentance.

There is no one righteous, not even one; there is no one who understands; there is no one who seeks God — Paul's catena of OT quotations in Romans 3 making the case for universal sinfulness stands on the foundation of Genesis 6:5. The totality language (every inclination... only evil... all the time) is the narrative form of the doctrinal universal that Paul argues theologically.

8.note: You have said, I know you by name and you have found favor with me — the first use of chen (grace/favor) in Scripture occurs here: Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD. The same vocabulary of finding favor (matsa chen) recurs when Moses pleads for divine presence; the grace vocabulary of the canon originates in Noah's exceptional standing before YHWH in a generation that was wholly corrupt.

9.note: By faith Noah, when warned about things not yet seen, in holy fear built an ark to save his family. By his faith he condemned the world and became heir of the righteousness that is in keeping with faith — Hebrews reads Noah's righteous and blameless walk (Genesis 6:9) through the lens of faith: the trust that acted on divine warning about what had never happened before is the faith that both saved his family and indicted the unbelieving generation.

Even if these three men — Noah, Daniel and Job — were in it, they could save only themselves by their righteousness — Ezekiel's triad of supremely righteous men places Noah first and uses his righteousness as the canonical standard for individual integrity. Genesis 6:9's description of Noah as righteous and blameless is the text Ezekiel is building on when he uses Noah as one of the three paradigms of righteousness.
Noah — righteous man preserved through flood

13.note: Noah builds the ark by faith
Noah saved through water — type of baptism that now saves you

18.note: I now establish my covenant with you and with your descendants after you — the first use of berit (covenant) in Scripture occurs in Genesis 6:18 as God announces what he will establish with Noah. The covenant vocabulary that structures the entire canon (Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, new covenant) has its lexical origin in this pre-flood promise to Noah.

The days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will make a new covenant with the people of Israel and Judah — Jeremiah's new covenant promise is intelligible against the full covenant trajectory that begins with Noah in Genesis 6:18. The covenant that preserves Noah through judgment anticipates the covenant that will preserve the new humanity through eschatological renewal.

1.note: By faith Abraham obeyed when he was called to go out to a place that he was to receive as an inheritance — the Abrahamic call and response (lekh-lekha) is interpreted in Hebrews as the paradigmatic faith-obedience: he went out, not knowing where he was going

Stephen's speech begins with the God of glory appearing to Abraham in Mesopotamia before he lived in Haran; the Abrahamic call inaugurates salvation history and is the foundational moment Stephen uses to show Israel's God as the God who works outside the land
Abraham's call to leave his homeland
Leave your country and people -- Stephen's speech
All families of earth blessed — fulfilled in Christ's resurrection

3.note: In you all the families of the earth shall be blessed — Paul reads the Abrahamic promise as the preaching of the gospel in advance: the Scripture foresaw that God would justify the Gentiles by faith and preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham; the blessing to all nations is fulfilled in the Gentile mission of Christ
All peoples on earth blessed through Abraham's offspring

18.note: Melchizedek priest of God Most High — type of Christ's priesthood
Priest in the order of Melchizedek — Jesus' eternal priesthood

Melchizedek is 'priest of God Most High' (kōhēn lĕ'ēl 'elyôn, Genesis 14:18) — the one OT figure who combines royal and priestly office before the Levitical system exists. Psalms 110:4 ('You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek') addresses the Davidic king in a way that Hebrews 7 applies solely to Christ: the Melchizedek-priesthood is the category that allows a king from Judah (not Levi) to exercise eternal priesthood.

Melchizedek appears without genealogy, without recorded birth or death — 'without father or mother or genealogy, having neither beginning of days nor end of life' (Hebrews 7:3). This is not a claim that Melchizedek was supernatural, but that the silence of the text, read typologically, makes him 'resembling the Son of God' who holds 'a permanent priesthood' (Hebrews 7:17) by virtue of indestructible life, not lineal succession.

19.note: Melchizedek brought out 'bread and wine' (leḥem wĕyayin) and blessed Abram (Genesis 14:18-19). The eucharistic elements appear here in a priestly blessing before the Levitical system, associated with the priest-king 'of the order of Melchizedek.' The Last Supper, where Jesus ('a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek,' Hebrews 7:17) takes bread and wine and blesses his disciples, fulfills this priestly type.

20.note: Abram gave Melchizedek 'a tenth of everything' (Genesis 14:20) — tithe to a priest before the Levitical law existed. Hebrews 7:4-10 makes this the basis for asserting Melchizedek's greatness over Levi: Levi was, in effect, 'still in the loins of his ancestor' Abraham when Abraham paid the tithe, meaning the Levitical priesthood is itself subordinate to the Melchizedekian priesthood that Christ fulfills.

5.note: Your offspring will be like the stars

'Look toward heaven, and number the stars, if you are able to number them... So shall your offspring be' (Genesis 15:5) — the promise is made to a man without heir. Galatians 3:29 applies the stellar-offspring promise to all who are in Christ: 'if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to promise.' The countless stars represent the multinational community of faith gathered in Christ.

6.note: Abraham believed God and it was counted to him as righteousness — Paul's foundational text for justification by faith; Genesis 15:6 is cited as the decisive proof that righteousness before God has always been by faith, not by works (since circumcision came later in Genesis 17)

Abraham believed God and it was counted to him as righteousness — Paul cites Genesis 15:6 to show that the blessing of Abraham comes to the Gentiles through faith, not through Torah-observance; the Galatian churches participate in the Abrahamic faith-righteousness
Abraham believed God — faith counted as righteousness
Abraham believed God — it was credited as righteousness
Abraham believed God — counted as righteousness

Abraham 'believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness' (Genesis 15:6) — this verse is the OT locus classicus for justification by faith, quoted in Romans 4:3, Galatians 3:6, and James 2:23. Its structure — faith in a divine promise about something not yet visible — matches Hebrews 11:1's definition: 'faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.'

14.note: They will serve as slaves -- Stephen on the exodus

1.note: 'Fear not, Abram, I am your shield (māgēn); your reward shall be very great' (Genesis 15:1) — the divine self-disclosure as shield to the patriarch who has renounced worldly reward (Genesis 14:22-23). The 'I am your shield' formula anticipates Christ's self-designation (John 10:11, 14: 'I am the good shepherd') as the one who absorbs the blow of judgment in the place of the flock.

17.note: The smoking fire pot and flaming torch that pass between the covenant pieces (Genesis 15:17) — God alone passes through, binding himself unilaterally. The fire is the theophanic form of the divine presence: the pillar of fire that leads Israel (Exodus 13:21-22), the burning bush (Exodus 3:2), the fire on Sinai (Exodus 19:18). The covenant ratified by fire anticipates the new covenant sealed by Christ's descent as the Spirit in tongues of fire (Acts 2:3).

The covenant of the pieces (Genesis 15:17) — cutting animals in half and walking between them — was the ANE covenant-ratification rite: the parties invoke upon themselves the fate of the divided animal if they break the covenant (cf. Jeremiah 34:18-19). Uniquely, only God passes through in Gen 15, making the covenant entirely unilateral: if it is broken, God himself bears the consequence. This is the typological background of the cross, where God in Christ bears the covenant curse (Galatians 3:13).

18.note: The formal covenant ratification 'on that day' (Genesis 15:18) — 'To your offspring I give this land' — is the covenant that Paul in Galatians 3:17 identifies as 430 years before the Law and therefore not annulled by the Law. The Abrahamic covenant's land promise stands because it was sealed by God's unilateral oath-act in Genesis 15:17, making it, like the new covenant, entirely a matter of grace rather than human performance.

10.note: Physical circumcision — spiritual circumcision in Christ
Sarah will have a son — promise at set time

1.note: Lot rescued from Sodom's destruction
Sodom and Gomorrah as examples of judgment
Sarah conceives Isaac in old age
Hagar and Sarah — two covenants

10.note: Get rid of the slave woman — Hagar cast out

12.note: Isaac — children of promise not natural descent

1.note: Abraham offers Isaac — faith in resurrection
Abraham justified by works — offering Isaac

8.note: God will provide for himself the lamb — the Aqedah (binding of Isaac): Abraham's willingness to sacrifice his only son is the OT type of God the Father giving his Son; 'God himself will provide the lamb' is fulfilled when God provides Christ as the Passover Lamb

By faith Abraham, when he was tested, offered up Isaac, and he who had received the promises was in the act of offering up his only son, of whom it was said: Through Isaac shall your offspring be named — he considered that God was able even to raise him from the dead; the Aqedah is interpreted as a resurrection-typology: Isaac's deliverance from death prefigured Christ's resurrection
God will provide the Lamb — Lamb of God who takes away sin

13.note: Isaac spared, ram in his place — God gave his only Son

17.note: I will surely bless you and give you many descendants

18.note: To Abraham and his Seed — meaning Christ

23.note: The older will serve the younger — Jacob and Esau

Pre-existing entry — 'the older will serve the younger' (Genesis 25:23) is Paul's proof text in Romans 9:10-12 for unconditional divine election: the oracle was given before the twins were born, before either had done good or evil, so that God's purpose of election would stand — not because of works but because of him who calls.

29.note: 'See to it that no one... is unholy like Esau, who sold his own birthright for a single meal' (Hebrews 12:16) — the birthright sale of Genesis 25:29-34 becomes in Hebrews the canonical image for trading ultimate inheritance for immediate gratification. The 'birthright' (bĕkōrāh) carried the double portion and patriarchal blessing; Esau's contempt for it ('What use is the birthright to me?' v. 32) is the OT type of those who profane holy things for temporal comfort.

34.note: 'Esau despised his birthright' (Genesis 25:34b) — Hebrews 12:17 completes the typological use: 'For you know that afterward, when he desired to inherit the blessing, he was rejected, for he found no chance to repent, though he sought it with tears.' The irreversibility of Esau's loss (confirmed in Genesis 27:38-40) makes him the canonical figure for the point of no return — the squandering of covenant privilege that cannot be undone by later regret.

27.note: Isaac blesses Jacob and Esau by faith

12.note: Jacob's ladder with angels ascending and descending — Jesus applies the Jacob's ladder vision to himself: you will see heaven opened and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man; Christ is the ladder/stairway between heaven and earth, the meeting point of divine and human

3.note: Father's beloved son rejected by brothers — Son loved by the Father

28.note: Joseph sold by his brothers for twenty pieces of silver — Stephen's speech identifies the Joseph narrative as a type of the rejection of Christ by his own people; as the patriarchs were jealous of Joseph and sold him, so they rejected Christ

Joseph sold for twenty pieces of silver (Genesis 37:28) — the price of a slave in the Mosaic law (Leviticus 27:5: the valuation of a male 5-20 years old). Jesus is betrayed for thirty pieces of silver (Matthew 26:15; Zechariah 11:12-13) — the price of a slave gored by an ox (Exodus 21:32). Both are 'slave-prices' — the contemptible valuation placed on the beloved son by those who sold him. The escalation from 20 to 30 and the Zechariah-Judas connection (Matthew 27:9-10) deepens the typological identification.

4.note: Joseph's brothers 'hated him and could not speak peaceably to him' (Genesis 37:4) — hatred without a rational cause, driven purely by envy of the father's favor. Jesus applies this pattern to himself and his disciples: 'if the world hates you, know that it has hated me before it hated you... they hated me without a cause' (John 15:18, 25, citing Psalms 35:19). The Joseph narrative structures the dynamic: divine favor provoking hatred from one's own family/people, leading to rejection and suffering that becomes the mechanism of salvation.

5.note: Joseph's first dream — sheaves bowing to his sheaf — and his second — the sun, moon, and eleven stars bowing to him (Genesis 37:9) — establish the prophetic pattern of cosmic imagery (sun, moon, stars) representing the covenant family. Revelation 12:1 ('a woman clothed with the sun, with the moon under her feet, and on her head a crown of twelve stars') directly absorbs this imagery: the twelve stars = the twelve tribes = the twelve apostles, making the connection between Joseph's prophetic dream and the church's cosmic identity explicit.

18.note: 'They conspired against him to kill him' (Genesis 37:18) — the brothers' murder-plot against Joseph uses the same conspiratorial pattern as the Sanhedrin's resolution against Jesus: 'they made plans to put him to death' (John 11:53). Psalms 2:2 ('the kings of the earth set themselves, and the rulers take counsel together, against the LORD and against his Anointed') gives the theological grammar: opposition to the chosen one arises from those who should recognize him, driven by the same fear-of-displacement that drove Joseph's brothers.

24.note: Joseph thrown into the pit — 'the pit was empty; there was no water in it' (Genesis 37:24) — the pit (bôr) is the Hebrew term for both cistern and the underworld/death (Psalms 28:1; Genesis 30:3; Genesis 88:4-6). Psalms 40:2 ('he drew me up from the pit of destruction, out of the miry bog') is David's language for deliverance from near-death, which the NT applies to Christ's resurrection (Hebrews 10:5-7 quotes Ps 40 of Christ). The empty waterless pit where Joseph is left to die is the type of the tomb from which the true Joseph is drawn up on the third day.

Joseph in the waterless pit (Genesis 37:24) is the structural forerunner of Jonah in the belly of the fish, who cries: 'you brought up my life from the pit (bôr)' (Jonah 2:6). Jesus explicitly identifies himself with Jonah: 'as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth' (Matthew 12:40). The pit-to-pit-to-tomb chain of types converges on the resurrection.

26.note: Judah (Hebrew form of the name Judas) proposes selling Joseph rather than killing him (Genesis 37:26-27) — 'let not our hand be upon him, for he is our brother, our own flesh.' Judas of Iscariot (from the tribe of Judah) proposes selling Jesus for thirty pieces of silver (Matthew 26:14-15). The verbal parallel — a 'Judah' figure brokering the sale of the beloved son — is structural: in both cases, the sale that seems to remove the threatened one becomes the mechanism of salvation for the very ones who arranged the sale (Acts 2:23: 'according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God').

36.note: Joseph sold to Potiphar in Egypt (Genesis 37:36) — the descent of the beloved, favored son into slavery in a foreign land begins the humiliation-exaltation arc that is the structural heart of the Joseph narrative. Paul's Christ-hymn follows the same arc: 'he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death... therefore God has highly exalted him' (Philippians 2:8-9). The Joseph type makes clear that the humiliation is not an accident but a necessary passage through which the exaltation comes — the pit precedes the throne.

39.note: Joseph exalted to rule over all — Christ exalted above every name

14.note: Joseph is brought out of the prison/dungeon (bôr, the pit) and brought before Pharaoh (Genesis 41:14) — the movement from death-place to royal presence is the exaltation arc in miniature. Acts 2:24 names the same movement for Christ: 'God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.' Joseph's emergence from the pit/prison is the type; Christ's resurrection from the tomb is the antitype — both are the vindication of the falsely condemned beloved son.

16.note: 'It is not in me; God will give Pharaoh a favorable answer' (Genesis 41:16) — Joseph refuses to take credit for the interpretation he is about to give. This consistent self-effacement in favor of the one who sent him is the pattern Jesus articulates in John 5:19: 'the Son can do nothing of his own accord, but only what he sees the Father doing' and Genesis 5:30: 'I can do nothing on my own... I seek not my own will but the will of him who sent me.' The true representative of God claims nothing for himself.

38.note: 'Can we find a man like this, in whom is the Spirit of God?' (Genesis 41:38) — Pharaoh's question marks Joseph as uniquely Spirit-endowed for a crisis-governance role. Isaiah 11:2 describes the Messiah: 'the Spirit of the LORD shall rest upon him, the Spirit of wisdom and understanding, the Spirit of counsel and might.' Luke 4:18 opens Jesus's ministry with the same pneumatic claim ('The Spirit of the Lord is upon me'). Joseph is the type; the Spirit-anointed servant-king is the archetype Isaiah prophesies and Jesus fulfills.

40.note: 'You shall be over my house, and all my people shall order themselves as you command' (Genesis 41:40) — Pharaoh's delegation to Joseph gives him universal executive authority in Egypt. Jesus's Great Commission claim — 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18) — is the cosmic scale of the same pattern: the exalted son given all authority by the one above him. Ephesians 1:20-22 makes the Christological application explicit: 'seated at his right hand in the heavenly places, far above all rule and authority... he put all things under his feet.'

43.note: Pharaoh's herald cries 'Bow the knee!' (ʾabrēk) before Joseph's chariot (Genesis 41:43) — a formal proclamation requiring public submission before the exalted ruler. Philippians 2:10-11 quotes Isaiah 45:23 and applies it to the exalted Christ: 'at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord.' Pharaoh's herald anticipates the cosmic herald who will announce the universal submission to the Lamb.

45.note: Joseph, the Hebrew slave elevated to viceroy, is given a Gentile wife, Asenath daughter of the priest of On (Genesis 41:45) — a Gentile bride for the covenant son who has become savior. Paul's description of the church in Ephesians 2:11-13 uses the same spatial movement: Gentiles 'once far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ.' The Gentile bride of the exalted Joseph types the church drawn from the nations as Christ's bride (Revelation 5:9: 'you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation').

55.note: When Egypt hungers, Pharaoh's answer is 'Go to Joseph; what he says to you, do' (Genesis 41:55) — the universal famine-solution is a single person to whom all are directed. John 6:35 records Jesus's self-identification as the universal answer to the hunger the famine represents: 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst.' Pharaoh's 'Go to Joseph' is the type of the Father's direction to the Son as the only source of life for the starving world.

57.note: 'All the earth came to Egypt to Joseph to buy grain, because the famine was severe over all the earth' (Genesis 41:57) — universal scope: the provision is sufficient for all who come. Jesus promises the same universality: 'I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself' (John 12:32). The cross-as-exaltation parallels Joseph's elevation: the one lifted up in apparent shame becomes the universal draw, the source of life for all who come.

31.note: Jacob worships leaning on his staff

7.note: Jacob blessed Pharaoh (Genesis 47:7) — the covenant-bearer (the weaker and older shepherd) blesses the world's most powerful ruler. Hebrews 7:7 states the principle: 'it is beyond dispute that the inferior is blessed by the superior.' The paradox is that Jacob, a nomadic alien with nothing in worldly terms, carries the superior covenant blessing that Pharaoh cannot confer on himself. The same paradox governs Christian mission: those who carry the gospel carry a blessing the world's powerful cannot provide for themselves (1 Corinthians 1:27-28).

9.note: 'The days of the years of my sojourning are 130 years. Few and evil have been the days of the years of my life' (Genesis 47:9) — Jacob's self-description as a sojourner (māgôr) at death's approach echoes the confession of all the patriarchs. Hebrews 11:13-16: 'These all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar, and having acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth... they desire a better country, that is, a heavenly one.' Peter applies the same identity to the church: 'sojourners and exiles' (1 Peter 2:11).

10.note: The scepter shall not depart from Judah nor the ruler's staff from between his feet, until tribute comes to him — the Lion of the tribe of Judah in Revelation is the fulfillment of Jacob's blessing on Judah; the royal authority of Genesis 49:10 is Christ's eschatological sovereignty
Lion of Judah, the scepter — the Lamb who is the conquering Lion
Scepter from Judah forever — throne of David given to Jesus

20.note: You meant evil — God meant it for good; deliberate plan of God

24.note: Joseph's dying command about his bones

1.note: Noah entering the ark 'by faith' to save his household structurally anticipates the pattern of the righteous remnant preserved through judgment — a type Hebrews explicitly applies: 'By faith Noah, being warned of God of things not seen as yet, moved with fear, prepared an ark to the saving of his house.'

The call to enter the ark before judgment falls is the narrative frame that 1 Peter 3:20-21 absorbs into its baptism typology — the invitation to safety precedes and enables survival through the waters of judgment.

7.note: Eight souls (oktō psychai) saved through water is the precise echo-point in 1 Peter 3:20-21: 'eight persons were brought safely through water. Baptism, which corresponds to this, now saves you.' The small number of the saved through water becomes the type for Christian baptism as passage through death into new life.

12.note: Forty days and forty nights of rain echo the recurring 40-day wilderness period: Moses on Sinai (Exodus 24:18; Genesis 34:28), Elijah's flight to Horeb (1 Kings 19:8), and Jesus's temptation (Matthew 4:2; Luke 4:2). The flood's 40 days launches the OT pattern of 40 as a period of trial, testing, or divine provision that culminates in Jesus's messianic temptation.

The rain that 'came upon the earth forty days and forty nights' (Genesis 7:12) and ultimately destroyed all life is the specific judgment event 2 Peter 3:6 cites as precedent for the final judgment by fire: 'the world that then existed was deluged with water and perished.'

11.note: The cosmological rupture — all the springs of the great deep bursting forth, floodgates of heaven opening — is the foundational judgment event that 2 Peter 3:5-7 invokes as the pattern for eschatological judgment: 'the world that then existed was deluged with water and perished... the heavens and earth that now exist are stored up for fire.'

21.note: The total perishing of all flesh outside the ark — 'all flesh died that moved on the earth' (Genesis 7:21) — is the annihilating scope that Jesus's eschatological discourse invokes: 'they were eating and drinking... until the day when Noah entered the ark, and the flood came and swept them all away. So will be the coming of the Son of Man' (Matthew 24:38-39; Luke 17:27).

23.note: Only Noah and those with him in the ark survived the destruction of 'every living thing on the face of the ground' (Genesis 7:23). Jesus uses this total destruction as the paradigm of sudden eschatological judgment that overtakes the unprepared, contrasting those inside (with the Son of Man) and those outside, swept away.

1.note: 'God remembered Noah' (wayyizkōr) — divine remembrance as the turning point from death to life. The vocabulary of God 'remembering' a person initiates restoration: cf. God remembering Rachel (Genesis 30:22), Hannah (1 Samuel 1:19), and Israel in slavery (Exodus 2:24). The thief's 'remember me when you come into your kingdom' (Luke 23:42) reaches for this same salvific remembrance.

The divine remembrance that ends the flood becomes the explicit type for God's covenant faithfulness in Isaiah 54:9: 'This is like the days of Noah to me: as I swore that the waters of Noah should no more go over the earth, so I have sworn that I will not be angry with you.'

8.note: The dove (yônāh) sent out over the waters of judgment as a scout for habitable land becomes the foundational type for the Holy Spirit descending as a dove at Jesus's baptism (Matthew 3:16; Mark 1:10; Luke 3:22; John 1:32). Both dove-events occur immediately after a passage through waters of judgment and mark the beginning of a new creation era.

11.note: The fresh olive leaf (ʿalêh-zayit) carried back by the dove signals that the judgment is past and peace with the ground is restored. Olive branches are the emblem of peace throughout Scripture; the dove's return with the olive leaf becomes a type of the 'peace with God' (Romans 5:1) that the Spirit announces and produces in those who have passed through baptismal death into resurrection life (cf. 1 Peter 3:20-21).

20.note: Noah's first act on dry ground is to build an altar and offer burnt offerings of every clean animal and bird (Genesis 8:20). The sacrifice that arises as a 'pleasing aroma' to the LORD foreshadows the sacrificial system that culminates in Christ's offering of himself (Ephesians 5:2: 'a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God'). The pattern — deliverance followed immediately by sacrifice and worship — recurs at the Passover, Sinai, and the cross.

21.note: 'I will never again curse the ground because of man' (Genesis 8:21) opens the Noahic covenant of common grace — the restraint of universal judgment that persists until the final reckoning. 2 Peter 3:9 interprets this patience: 'The Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish.'

The fixed order of 'seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night' (Genesis 8:22) as God's covenant guarantee becomes the basis for Jeremiah's 'fixed order' argument for the certainty of God's covenant promises (Jeremiah 33:20-21, 25-26) — as surely as day follows night, so God's covenant with David will stand.

1.note: 'Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth' (Genesis 9:1) re-issues the original creation mandate (Genesis 1:28) to the post-flood humanity, marking Noah as a new-Adam figure beginning creation afresh. The Great Commission (Matthew 28:19-20) echoes this post-resurrection new-creation mandate: Christ, the last Adam (1 Corinthians 15:45), sends his people to 'fill the earth' with disciples in the power of the Spirit.

3.note: 'Every moving thing that lives shall be food for you' (Genesis 9:3) grants Noah's post-flood humanity a broader food permission than Eden's (Genesis 1:29-30). Jesus's declaration that all foods are clean (Mark 7:19) and Peter's vision ('Rise, kill and eat' — Acts 10:13-15) fulfill this trajectory, removing the Levitical distinctions that had qualified the Noahic permission for Israel.

Mark glosses Jesus's teaching on defilement with the editorial note 'thus he declared all foods clean' (Mark 7:19), presenting this as a fulfillment and clarification of the principle established in Genesis 9:3 — the post-flood permission for all flesh — against which Levitical distinctions were a temporary, pedagogical narrowing.

6.note: 'For God made man in his own image' (Genesis 9:6) grounds the prohibition of murder in the imago Dei — human dignity is not self-generated but derived from divine imaging. Paul's Areopagus speech echoes this (Acts 17:26-28): all humanity from one man, bearing the divine image, is accountable to the one who made them. Christ as the 'image of the invisible God' (Colossians 1:15) restores and fulfills the image.

The lex talionis of Genesis 9:6 ('by man shall his blood be shed') establishes that the authority over life and death belongs to God, delegated to human courts. Jesus acknowledges this in John 19:11 ('you would have no authority over me at all unless it had been given you from above') — the judicial killing at the cross operates within the same divinely delegated framework that 9:6 establishes.

9.note: God's covenant with Noah (Genesis 9:9-11) is one of several foundational divine covenants (Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, New) that form the backbone of redemptive history. Hebrews 6:13-20 connects God's covenant oath-making to Christ as 'a sure and steadfast anchor of the soul, a hope that enters into the inner place behind the curtain' — every divine covenant oath reaches its 'Yes' in Christ (2 Corinthians 1:20).

11.note: 'Never again shall all flesh be cut off by the waters of a flood' (Genesis 9:11) — the covenant promise that common grace will persist until the appointed end. Paul's claim that 'all the promises of God find their Yes in him [Christ]' (2 Corinthians 1:20) includes this foundational promise: the patience of God in sustaining creation despite human sin is itself a covenant mercy, sustained by Christ, who was appointed heir of all things (Hebrews 1:2).

12.note: The rainbow (qešet, Genesis 9:12-13) set in the cloud as the sign of the Noahic covenant reappears around the throne of God in John's vision (Revelation 4:3: 'a rainbow that had the appearance of an emerald encircled the throne') and on the angel with a little scroll (Revelation 10:1). The rainbow migrates from sign of past judgment's end to emblem of the sovereign mercy that governs all things from the divine throne.

13.note: The rainbow in the cloud (Genesis 9:13) is the covenant sign that God will not again destroy all life — God's restraint of judgment upon a sinful world. Revelation 10:1 shows a mighty angel 'wrapped in a cloud, with a rainbow over his head' — the covenant mercy of 9:13 is carried into the eschatological judgment scene as the assurance that God's judgments remain bounded by covenant faithfulness.

16.note: 'The everlasting covenant between God and every living creature' (Genesis 9:16) uses the phrase bĕrît ʿôlām (everlasting/eternal covenant) — the same formula applied to the Abrahamic (Genesis 17:7), Mosaic (Leviticus 24:8), Davidic (2 Samuel 23:5), and New Covenant (Jeremiah 32:40; Ezekiel 37:26) commitments. Hebrews 13:20 identifies Christ's resurrection as the ratification of 'the eternal covenant' by 'the blood of the eternal covenant,' drawing this entire covenant chain into Christ.

26.note: 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem' (Genesis 9:26) — the blessing on Shem initiates the redemptive genealogical trajectory that runs through Shem → Eber → Terah → Abraham → Isaac → Jacob → Judah → David → Christ. Luke 3:36 includes Shem explicitly in Christ's genealogy, anchoring the messianic line in the post-flood table of nations precisely at the point where Noah's blessing narrowed the promise.

1.note: The Table of Nations (Gen 10) is the canonical background to Paul's Areopagus declaration: 'From one man he made all the nations (panta ta ethnē) to live on all the face of the earth, having determined allotted periods and the boundaries of their dwelling place.' Paul draws directly on the Noahic-nations framework to establish that all ethnic diversity descends from a single humanity made for God.

The 70 nations of the Table of Nations (counted in Hebrew tradition as 70 from Shem, Ham, and Japheth) are the world that every-nation-tribe-people-language of Revelation 7:9 reverses: where Genesis 10 describes the scattering into peoples, Revelation's great multitude is the eschatological gathering of every scattered people before the Lamb.

5.note: The coastland peoples spread 'each with his own language, by their clans, in their nations' (Genesis 10:5) — an anticipation of the Babel fragmentation (Genesis 11) and its eschatological reversal at Pentecost, where each person heard the apostles speaking 'in his own language' (glōssē); the Spirit at Pentecost undoes what the nations' scattering established.

8.note: Nimrod, 'a mighty hunter before the LORD' and the first kingdom-builder of Babel (Genesis 10:8-10), is the OT seed of the Babylon-the-Great typology. His founding of Babel (the city of confusion, Genesis 11) becomes the enduring symbol of human empire against God — Revelation's Babylon is Nimrod's project at its eschatological extreme.

10.note: Nimrod's first kingdom was 'Babel, Erech, Akkad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar' (Genesis 10:10). The name Babel (Babylon in Greek) becomes the NT's code for the final human empire opposed to God: Revelation 17:5 labels 'Babylon the Great' as the mother of harlots. The river begins here in Gen 10 — Nimrod's Shinar, the Tower of Babel (Genesis 11), Israel's captivity, and Rev 18's lament are all within the same Babylon typology.

25.note: Eber's son Peleg ('for in his days the earth was divided,' Genesis 10:25) is the pivot point of the Table of Nations — the division of peoples happens in Peleg's era (corresponding to Babel, Gen 11). Peleg appears in Luke's genealogy of Jesus (Luke 3:35), tracing the Savior's lineage through this very point of division, as if to mark him as the one who will gather what Peleg's era scattered.

32.note: The Table of Nations concludes: 'From these the nations spread abroad on the earth after the flood' (Genesis 10:32). This founding dispersal of the nations is the backdrop against which God calls Abram (Genesis 12:1-3) to be the channel through whom all these nations will be blessed. Paul's citation of Genesis 12:3 in Galatians 3:8 ('the Scripture foresaw that God would justify the Gentiles by faith') makes sense only against the Table of Nations — God was always working toward all these nations.

1.note: The pre-Babel world had 'one language and the same words' (Genesis 11:1) — linguistic unity marking undivided humanity. Pentecost partially reverses this: where Babel shattered the one language into many (Genesis 11:7), the Spirit at Pentecost did not restore a single language but enabled each to hear in their own language (Acts 2:6-8), a deeper unity-in-diversity that prefigures Revelation 7:9's eschatological gathering.

4.note: 'Let us build ourselves a city and a tower with its top in the heavens, and let us make a name for ourselves, lest we be dispersed over the face of the whole earth' (Genesis 11:4) — the Babel project is the archetype of every human city that exalts itself against God. Revelation's lament over Babylon ('Alas, alas, O great city Babylon, you mighty city!' Revelation 18:10) is the final reckoning with what Babel began: the city built to make a name, brought down in an hour.

7.note: YHWH's verdict at Babel — 'Come, let us go down and there confuse their language, so that they may not understand one another's speech' (Genesis 11:7) — is the founding act of human ethnic and linguistic division. The Pentecost reversal is exact: where YHWH 'confused' (bālal) Babel, the Spirit at Pentecost gave speech that each person understood; what was scattered is being regathered through the gospel (Acts 2:4-8).

9.note: Babel — 'because there YHWH confused (bālal) the language of all the earth, and from there YHWH dispersed them over the face of all the earth' — gives its name to Babylon, which the prophets (Isa 13; Jer 50-51) and Revelation use as the symbol of human empire's end. Revelation's 'Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great' (Revelation 14:8; Genesis 18:2) is the final judgment on what the builders of Babel set in motion.

Peter's closing greeting 'She who is at Babylon, who is likewise chosen, sends you greetings' (1 Peter 5:13) uses Babel/Babylon as a code name for Rome — showing that Babel became a canonical type for every empire that sets itself against the people of God. The typology began in Genesis 11:9 and was still alive in Peter's use of it decades after Christ's ascension.

10.note: The Shem-to-Terah genealogy (Genesis 11:10-26) — Shem, Arpachshad, Shelah, Eber, Peleg, Reu, Serug, Nahor, Terah — is reproduced in Luke's genealogy of Jesus (Luke 3:35-36), demonstrating that the Lucan Christ comes through the Shemite covenant line that runs from Noah's son Shem through to Abraham. The genealogy is not merely biographical; it traces the covenant line forward toward the seed of Abraham.

26.note: Terah fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran (Genesis 11:26-27), setting up the Abrahamic call of Genesis 12. Stephen's speech (Acts 7:2) dates the divine appearance to Abraham to his time in Mesopotamia before he settled in Haran — identifying the God who called Abraham as the God of glory, the covenant-keeping God who works outside the land, as he will work among the Gentile nations.

31.note: Terah took Abram, Lot, and Sarai from Ur of the Chaldeans to go to Canaan but settled in Haran (Genesis 11:31). The journey begun by Terah that he did not complete becomes the journey Abraham completes by faith (Hebrews 11:8: 'he went out, not knowing where he was going'). The staging of the call at Haran (after the aborted journey from Ur) underscores the point that Abraham is not acting on his own initiative but responding to divine command.

10.note: Lot 'saw that the Jordan valley was well watered everywhere like the garden of the LORD' (Genesis 13:10) — the Edenic lushness of the valley is the surface appeal that conceals its proximity to Sodom. The New Jerusalem's river of life and tree of life (Revelation 22:1-2) restore the genuine garden of God that Lot's choice only approximated and perverted.

14.note: 'Lift up your eyes and look from the place where you are, northward and southward and eastward and westward, for all the land that you see I will give to you and to your offspring forever' (Genesis 13:14-15) — the promise of visible land to unseen offspring is the structure of Abrahamic faith. Hebrews 11:8-10 reads this promise as looking beyond Canaan to 'the city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God.'

15.note: 'To your offspring I will give it' (Genesis 13:15) — Paul's exegesis in Galatians 3:16 turns on the singular 'offspring' (sperma): it does not say 'offsprings' (plural) but 'offspring' (singular), who is Christ. The land promise to Abraham's singular Seed is thus ultimately a promise fulfilled in and through Christ, not merely ethnic Israel.

The comprehensive land promise — 'all the land that you see' — is interpreted by Paul in Romans 4:13 as a promise that Abraham would be 'heir of the world (kosmos),' not merely of Canaan. This universalization of the land promise occurs through faith, not law, and is inherited by those who share Abraham's faith in the God who gives life to the dead.

16.note: 'I will make your offspring as the dust of the earth, so that if one can count the dust of the earth, your offspring also can be counted' (Genesis 13:16) — the hyperbolic promise to an childless man. Hebrews 11:12 holds this promise alongside the starlike-multitude promise (Genesis 15:5): 'from one man, and him as good as dead, were born descendants as many as the stars of heaven and as many as the innumerable grains of sand by the seashore.'

18.note: Abram moved his tent to Hebron and 'built there an altar to the LORD' (Genesis 13:18) — the pattern of faithful sojourn punctuated by altar-building (cf. Genesis 12:7, 8; Genesis 13:4, 18; Genesis 26:25; Genesis 35:1-7). The pilgrim-patriarch building altars to God in foreign land foreshadows the church's calling to offer 'sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ' (1 Peter 2:5) while sojourning in a world that is not yet the homeland (Hebrews 11:13-16).

1.note: Sarai's barrenness and her Egyptian maidservant Hagar (Genesis 16:1) are the two women of Paul's explicit allegory in Galatians 4:22-31: 'Abraham had two sons, one by a slave woman and one by a free woman.' Paul identifies Hagar with the Sinai covenant (and the law), Sarai/Sarah with the Jerusalem above (and the new covenant of promise). The historical account thus carries a typological freight Paul describes as an allegory about two covenant orders.

3.note: Sarai gives Hagar to Abram so that she 'may obtain children by her' (Genesis 16:3) — the attempt to fulfill the divine promise by human arrangement rather than divine gift. This is the 'born after the flesh' pattern (Galatians 4:23) that Paul contrasts with Isaac 'born through promise' (Galatians 4:23). The attempt to secure the covenant blessing through human strategy is the fundamental error that the law-covenant repeats — seeking righteousness by works rather than receiving it by promise.

7.note: The angel of the LORD finds Hagar 'by a spring of water in the wilderness, the spring on the way to Shur' (Genesis 16:7) — the pattern of the divine messenger seeking out the outcast and marginalized in the wilderness recurs throughout Luke-Acts. Luke's Gospel emphasizes God's reach to those outside the covenant community (Samaritans, Gentiles, women) in language that echoes this foundational scene where God pursues the runaway slave girl.

10.note: 'I will surely multiply your offspring so that they cannot be numbered for multitude' (Genesis 16:10) — the multiplication promise given to Hagar echoes the promise to Abraham (Genesis 13:16; Genesis 15:5). Romans 9:7-9 distinguishes the two lineages: 'not all children of Abraham are his offspring' — the promise runs through Isaac (the child of the free woman), not Ishmael. The parallel promises reveal that God's purpose of election, not physical descent, governs who inherits the covenant.

11.note: The angel's naming oracle — 'You shall call his name Ishmael, because the LORD has listened (šāmaʿ) to your affliction' (Genesis 16:11) — follows the pattern of angelic annunciation: angel appears → name given → etymology explained. Luke 1:31 ('you will conceive... and you shall call his name Jesus') and Genesis 1:13 (Zechariah: 'call his name John') repeat this naming-oracle structure, placing the incarnation within a long line of divine interventions announced by heavenly messengers.

13.note: 'You are a God of seeing' — ʾattāh ʾēl roʾî, the name Hagar gives God (Genesis 16:13). The marginalized slave woman outside the covenant community is the one who receives divine vision and gives God a new name. Mary's Magnificat echoes this structure: 'he has looked on the humble estate of his servant' (Luke 1:48 — epeblepsen, he looked attentively) — the God who saw Hagar is the God who sees the lowly and reverses their condition.

'The LORD, the God who sees me' (El Roi, Genesis 16:13) — the divine attribute of seeing those whom humans overlook. Psalms 34:15 ('The eyes of the LORD are toward the righteous and his ears toward their cry') develops this theme. Christ, who 'saw' the widow of Nain in her grief (Luke 7:13), the Zacchaeus hiding in the sycamore tree (Luke 19:5), and the bleeding woman in the crowd (Mark 5:32), embodies the El Roi character of God.

1.note: The Abimelech episode endangers Sarah — and therefore the promised seed — for the second time (the first is Genesis 12:10-20 in Egypt). God sovereignly intervenes before Sarah is taken, preserving the Abrahamic line through which Isaac must come. Paul's 'through Isaac shall your offspring be named' (Romans 9:7) presupposes that this line was protected despite Abraham's failures; the promise-seed is God's doing, not Abraham's.

7.note: God tells Abimelech: 'He is a prophet (nāḇî), and he will pray for you' — this is the first occurrence of the word nāḇî (prophet) in the OT. The prophet's role here is intercessory: God speaks through Abraham to Abimelech, and Abraham prays for healing. Numbers 12:6 defines the prophetic office ('If there is a prophet among you, I the LORD make myself known to him in a vision') — Abraham's nocturnal divine encounter and intercessory prayer establish the prototype.

Abraham's prayer for Abimelech (v. 7, fulfilled in v. 17) foreshadows Christ's permanent intercessory ministry: 'he always lives to make intercession for them' (Hebrews 7:25). Abraham as prophet-intercessor is one of the OT shadows of the one Mediator who prays for those who come to God through him (1 Timothy 2:5).

12.note: Abraham's disclosure that Sarah is indeed his half-sister (Genesis 20:12) — true, but misleading — is part of the narrative context that makes the divine protection of Sarah all the more striking. The Matthean genealogy traces Jesus's line through Abraham (Matthew 1:1-2); the protection of Sarah in Gerar, like the protection in Egypt, is the protection of the Messianic line at its most vulnerable.

17.note: Abraham prayed to God and God healed Abimelech — the fulfilled intercession of Genesis 20:17 grounds the principle James articulates: 'the prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working' (James 5:16). Abraham is the paradigmatic righteous man whose prayer effects healing for a Gentile king, across the ethnic boundary that will only be fully broken down in Christ (Galatians 3:28).

1.note: Sarah's death at Hebron (v. 1-2) opens the Machpelah purchase narrative, which is the canonical expression of the patriarchs' alien-and-stranger status: 'These all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar, and having acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth' (Hebrews 11:13). Sarah is buried in the promised land without possessing it; the faith of the patriarchs consists precisely in this — dying without the promise fulfilled, but trusting the God who promised.

4.note: Abraham's request — 'I am a sojourner and foreigner (gēr-wĕtôšāb) among you; give me property among you for a burying place' (v. 4) — is the exact situation Stephen cites in Acts 7:5: 'He gave him no inheritance in it, not even a foot's length, but promised to give it to him as a possession and to his offspring after him, when as yet he had no child.' Stephen reads Machpelah as proof that the patriarchal promise was not yet fulfilled in Abraham's lifetime; the land awaits its full-inheritance recipient.

9.note: The cave of Machpelah purchased for 400 shekels of silver (v. 9-16) is the patriarchs' only concrete possession in Canaan — a tomb, not a homestead. This 'down payment' on the land is theologically resonant with Paul's description of the Spirit as 'the guarantee (arrabōn) of our inheritance' (Ephesians 1:14): the Spirit in this age is what Machpelah was for Abraham — the purchased first installment of the full inheritance that awaits the resurrection and new creation.

19.note: Abraham buries Sarah in the cave of Machpelah, in Hebron, in the land of Canaan (v. 19-20) — the patriarchs' dead are in the ground of the land they were promised. Hebrews 11:16 draws the theological conclusion: 'They desire a better country, that is, a heavenly one. Therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God, for he has prepared for them a city.' Machpelah is not the goal — it is the evidence of hope fixed on a city and country beyond what Abraham could possess in his lifetime.

2.note: Abraham makes his servant swear 'by the LORD, the God of heaven and of earth' (Genesis 24:3) — a covenant oath that binds the entire mission. Hebrews 6:13-17 develops the logic of divine oath-making: 'since there was no one greater than himself, he swore by himself.' The whole of Gen 24 turns on an oath freely given, structuring a mission of grace — the pattern behind the new covenant mission of the Spirit.

4.note: Abraham's insistence that his son's wife must not be taken from the Canaanites but from his own kindred (Genesis 24:4) — the bride must come from the covenant community, those who share the lineage of promise. Ephesians 5:27 uses the same logic to describe the church as the bride Christ presents to himself 'without spot or wrinkle or any such thing... holy and without blemish' — the bride's identity is defined by her relation to the Bridegroom's heritage.

7.note: 'He will send his angel before you' (Genesis 24:7) — Abraham's confidence that God will guide the servant's mission echoes through every sending commission in Scripture. Jesus's Great Commission promise 'I am with you always, to the end of the age' (Matthew 28:20) is the NT counterpart: the Son sends the Spirit (John 15:26; Genesis 16:7) and accompanies the mission the way God accompanies the servant in Gen 24.

12.note: The servant's prayer at the well — 'Please grant me success today' (Genesis 24:12) — is the model of Spirit-guided mission prayer: asking for divine direction in the work of finding the bride. Jesus describes the Spirit's mission in exactly these terms: 'he will guide you into all truth... he will glorify me' (John 16:13-14). The servant throughout Gen 24 refuses to speak of himself; the Spirit likewise does not speak of himself (John 16:13).

14.note: The servant sets a specific sign by which he will recognize the chosen bride: her willingness to draw water not just for him but for all his camels (Genesis 24:14). This tests for generous, generous-beyond-what-is-asked service — the character of the one called. Similarly, the Spirit seals those who hear and believe (Ephesians 1:13-14), recognizing and ratifying the response of faith, not imposing it coercively.

15.note: 'Before he had finished speaking, behold, Rebekah... came out' (Genesis 24:15) — the answer to prayer arrives before the prayer is complete, a mark of divine foreknowledge and provision already in motion. Isaiah 65:24: 'Before they call I will answer; while they are yet speaking I will hear' — the pattern of preemptive divine grace that grounds NT prayer confidence (Matthew 6:8: 'your Father knows what you need before you ask him').
